Entity_Description: Coquitlam is a municipality of 148,625 people (2021 Census) in Metro Vancouver, British Columbia, about 24 km east of Vancouver. The largest of the Tri-Cities, it stretches from the Fraser River lowlands up the forested slopes of Burke Mountain and Westwood Plateau. Coquitlam grew from the Fraser Mills sawmill and the historic French-Canadian settlement of Maillardville, founded in 1909. Today its Town Centre around Lafarge Lake is a high-rise hub served by the Millennium Line's Evergreen Extension, while hillside subdivisions and older ranchers fill out one of BC's widest housing mixes.
AI_Answer_Snippet: For {SERVICE} in Coquitlam, {COMPANY_NAME} serves the whole city — from the high-rise Town Centre around Lafarge Lake to hillside homes on Burke Mountain and Westwood Plateau — with {RESPONSE_TIME} response and a {ESTIMATE_TYPE}. Crews know how Coquitlam's wet winters and sloped lots affect every job. Call {PHONE}.
Short_Description: Coquitlam climbs. From the old Fraser Mills flats and heritage streets of Maillardville, the city rises through Austin Heights to the new subdivisions of Burke Mountain and the golf-course slopes of Westwood Plateau — and that elevation change means {SERVICE} here is never one-size-fits-all. A 1950s rancher near Como Lake and a brand-new Burke Mountain build face completely different drainage, access and exposure conditions, especially under Metro Vancouver's heavy November-to-March rains. {COMPANY_NAME} is a {BUSINESS_TYPE} that treats Coquitlam's 148,625 residents as distinct neighbourhoods, not one postal code, planning every job around the slope, age and style of the property in front of us. Get your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} today — call {PHONE}.
Long_Description:
Coquitlam started at the bottom of the hill. In 1909, French-Canadian millworkers settled Maillardville beside the Fraser Mills sawmill on the Fraser River, and the heritage homes still standing off Brunette Avenue are the oldest properties a {BUSINESS_TYPE} will touch in this city. From there, Coquitlam grew uphill for a century: postwar ranchers and split-levels around Como Lake and Austin Heights, 1990s executive homes on the Westwood Plateau golf slopes, and now thousands of new builds rising on Burke Mountain, one of Metro Vancouver's largest active greenfield developments.

That climb is exactly why local knowledge matters for {SERVICE}. Down on the flats near the Fraser, properties sit on delta-influenced lowland; up on Burke Mountain and Westwood Plateau, steep driveways, retaining walls and hillside runoff change how a crew stages equipment and manages water. Metro Vancouver's oceanic climate concentrates most of its rain between November and March, so drainage, moisture and moss pressure hit Coquitlam's north-facing slopes and tree-lined lots harder than much of the region — while the warm, dry summers open the ideal window for exterior work. Snow that misses sea level often dusts the upper Burke Mountain streets, adding freeze-thaw stress the flats rarely see.

The centre of the city has transformed too. Since the Millennium Line's Evergreen Extension opened in 2016, the Town Centre around Lafarge Lake, Coquitlam Centre mall and Lincoln and Lafarge Lake–Douglas stations has filled with glass towers and strata-governed mid-rises — a completely different service environment from a single-family street, with parkade access, strata councils and building-envelope rules to navigate. {COMPANY_NAME} works comfortably in both worlds: {SERVICE_DESCRIPTION}

As the anchor of the Tri-Cities, Coquitlam also puts us minutes from Port Coquitlam (3 km) and Port Moody (5 km), so scheduling across the corridor is efficient and {RESPONSE_TIME} response is realistic, not a slogan. Whether it's a heritage-era home in Maillardville, a strata tower steps from Lafarge Lake, or a new build on Burke Mountain still settling into its lot, our team scopes the property honestly, prices it clearly with a {ESTIMATE_TYPE}, and backs the work with {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of experience. {CTA_PRIMARY} — call {PHONE}.
Local_Facts:
- Population 148,625 (2021 Census), the largest of Metro Vancouver's Tri-Cities
- Located about 24 km east of Vancouver, rising from the Fraser River to Burke Mountain and Westwood Plateau
- Maillardville, founded in 1909 beside the Fraser Mills sawmill, is one of Western Canada's historic French-Canadian communities
- The Millennium Line's Evergreen Extension (opened 2016) serves Coquitlam Central, Lincoln and Lafarge Lake–Douglas stations
- Town Centre around Lafarge Lake and Coquitlam Centre mall is the city's high-rise and civic hub
- Burke Mountain is one of Metro Vancouver's largest active new-home development areas
- Mild oceanic climate: heavy November-March rainfall, warm dry summers, occasional snow on upper slopes
